era_context: The American Civil War was in its second year, with the conflict becoming increasingly brutal and transformative. European powers were consolidating nationalist movements and imperial ambitions. The Industrial Revolution was accelerating technological change. The Second Opium War had recently concluded, and the Taiping Rebellion was ongoing in China.

events.title: Battle of the Monitor and Merrimack (CSS Virginia)
events.description: The Battle of Hampton Roads, fought on March 8–9, 1862, between the USS Monitor and the CSS Virginia (formerly USS Merrimack), was the first naval battle between ironclad warships. The Confederate ironclad CSS Virginia was launched on March 8, 1862, and immediately attacked Union wooden warships, destroying several. The Union ironclad USS Monitor arrived on March 9, 1862, and engaged the CSS Virginia in a historic battle that lasted several hours. Although the battle was tactically inconclusive, it demonstrated the obsolescence of wooden warships and revolutionised naval warfare. The battle had enormous strategic significance, as it prevented the CSS Virginia from breaking the Union blockade of Confederate ports. The battle marked the beginning of the age of ironclad warships and fundamentally changed naval technology.
key_figures: Secretary of the Navy Gideon Welles

sources.name: Official Union Navy records and reports
Confederate Navy records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts (New York Times, Richmond Examiner, etc.)
Eyewitness accounts from naval officers and sailors

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Hampton Roads is extensively documented in naval records and contemporary accounts. Dates and outcomes are well-established.

events.title: Battle of Shiloh (American Civil War)
events.description: The Battle of Shiloh, fought on April 6–7, 1862, in Tennessee, was one of the bloodiest battles of the American Civil War up to that point. Union General Ulysses S. Grant's Army of the Tennessee was surprised by Confederate General Albert Sidney Johnston's attack on April 6, 1862, but Union reinforcements arrived and the Confederates were repulsed. The battle resulted in approximately 24,000 casualties, shocking the American public with the scale of death and destruction. The battle demonstrated that the Civil War would be a long, brutal conflict rather than a quick Union victory. The battle also established Grant's reputation as a determined commander willing to accept heavy casualties to achieve victory. The battle's scale and brutality foreshadowed the devastating nature of the remaining Civil War campaigns.
key_figures: General Albert Sidney Johnston (Confederate commander, killed in battle)
General Don Carlos Buell (Union reinforcement commander)
General P.G.T. Beauregard (Confederate commander after Johnston's death)

sources.name: Official Union Army records and Grant's memoirs
Confederate military records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Shiloh is extensively documented in military records and contemporary accounts. Dates and casualty figures are well-established.

events.title: Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation issued by Lincoln
events.description: President Abraham Lincoln issued the Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation on September 22, 1862, following the Union victory at the Battle of Antietam. The proclamation declared that enslaved people in Confederate states would be freed on January 1, 1863, unless the states returned to the Union. The preliminary proclamation was a military measure intended to weaken the Confederacy and encourage enslaved people to flee to Union lines. The proclamation was controversial in the North, with some supporting it as a necessary war measure and others opposing it as an overreach of presidential power. The preliminary proclamation set the stage for the final Emancipation Proclamation issued on January 1, 1863.

sources.name: Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation text and official government documents
Lincoln's correspondence and speeches
Contemporary newspaper accounts and political journals

events.certainty_note: The Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation is documented in official government records and contemporary accounts. The date (September 22, 1862) is confirmed.

events.title: Battle of Antietam (American Civil War)
events.description: The Battle of Antietam, fought on September 17, 1862, in Maryland, was the bloodiest single day in American military history, with approximately 23,000 casualties. Union General George B. McClellan's Army of the Potomac fought Confederate General Robert E. Lee's Army of Northern Virginia to a tactical draw, but Lee withdrew from Maryland, ending his invasion of the North. Although tactically inconclusive, the battle was strategically significant as a Union victory that prevented Confederate invasion of the North and gave President Lincoln the opportunity to issue the Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation. The battle demonstrated the enormous cost of the Civil War and shocked the American public with the scale of death and destruction.

sources.name: Official Union Army records and McClellan's reports
Confederate military records and Lee's correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts and soldier letters
Photographs by Mathew Brady (first photographs of a battlefield)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Antietam is extensively documented in military records and contemporary accounts. The date (September 17, 1862) and casualty figures are well-established.

events.title: Second French Intervention in Mexico begins
events.description: French military forces, supported by Spanish and British troops, invaded Mexico in 1862 to enforce debt repayment and establish a European-style monarchy. The intervention was led by French Emperor Napoleon III, who sought to expand French influence in the Americas and establish a client state in Mexico. The initial French invasion was repulsed at the Battle of Puebla on May 5, 1862, by Mexican forces under General Ignacio Zaragoza, but the French eventually prevailed and occupied Mexico City in 1863. The Second French Intervention lasted until 1867 and resulted in the installation of Archduke Maximilian of Austria as Emperor of Mexico. The intervention was controversial in Europe and the United States and ultimately failed when French forces withdrew and Mexican republicans under Benito Juárez restored the republic.

sources.name: French military records and diplomatic correspondence
Mexican government records and military reports
Contemporary newspaper accounts and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Second French Intervention in Mexico is documented in French and Mexican records and contemporary accounts. The invasion began in 1862.

events.title: Battle of Puebla (Mexico)
events.description: The Battle of Puebla was fought on May 5, 1862, between Mexican forces under General Ignacio Zaragoza and French forces under General Charles Lorencez. The Mexican forces, despite being outnumbered and less well-equipped, defeated the French invasion force, forcing the French to retreat. The Mexican victory was celebrated as a major triumph and boosted Mexican national morale. However, the French eventually returned with reinforcements and conquered Mexico, occupying Mexico City in 1863. The Battle of Puebla is commemorated in Mexico as Cinco de Mayo (May 5), a national holiday celebrating Mexican resistance to foreign intervention. Although the battle was ultimately not decisive in the outcome of the French intervention, it became a symbol of Mexican national pride and resistance.

sources.name: Mexican military records and government documents
French military records and correspondence
Contemporary newspaper accounts

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Puebla is documented in Mexican and French military records and contemporary accounts. The date (May 5, 1862) is confirmed.

events.title: Dakota War (Sioux Uprising) in Minnesota
events.description: The Dakota War (also called the Sioux Uprising) began in August 1862 in Minnesota, when Dakota Sioux warriors, led by Little Crow, rose up against U.S. settlers and soldiers. The uprising was triggered by broken treaties, delayed annuity payments, and the encroachment of settlers on Dakota lands. The conflict lasted several months and resulted in the deaths of hundreds of settlers and soldiers, as well as thousands of Dakota people. The uprising was ultimately suppressed by U.S. military forces, and many Dakota people were killed, displaced, or executed. The Dakota War resulted in the further marginalisation of Dakota people and the loss of their lands in Minnesota. The conflict is part of the broader pattern of Indian Wars in the 19th century.

sources.name: U.S. Army records and military reports
Dakota oral traditions and later ethnographic accounts
Contemporary newspaper accounts and settler accounts

events.certainty_note: The Dakota War is documented in U.S. military records and contemporary accounts. The uprising began in August 1862.

events.title: Homestead Act signed into law
events.description: President Abraham Lincoln signed the Homestead Act into law on May 20, 1862. The act provided 160 acres of public land to settlers who would improve the land and live on it for five years. The Homestead Act was a major piece of legislation that encouraged westward expansion and settlement of the American frontier. The act resulted in the distribution of millions of acres of public land to settlers, many of whom were immigrants. However, the act also resulted in the displacement of Native American peoples from their lands, as settlers expanded westward. The Homestead Act was one of the most significant pieces of legislation in American history, shaping the settlement and development of the American West.
key_figures: Representative Justin Smith Morrill (sponsor of the act)
Secretary of the Interior Caleb Blood Smith

sources.name: Homestead Act text and official government documents
Congressional records and legislative documents
Contemporary newspaper accounts

events.certainty_note: The Homestead Act is documented in official government records and congressional documents. The date (May 20, 1862) is confirmed.

events.title: Taiping Rebellion continues in China
events.description: The Taiping Rebellion, which had begun in 1851, continued in 1862 with ongoing warfare between Taiping forces and Qing imperial forces. The rebellion had resulted in millions of deaths and the destruction of vast areas of China. In 1862, the Qing Dynasty, with assistance from foreign military advisers and weapons, began to gain the upper hand against the Taiping forces. The rebellion would be finally suppressed in 1864 with the fall of Nanjing. The Taiping Rebellion was one of the deadliest conflicts of the 19th century and had profound effects on Chinese society and the Qing Dynasty's ability to resist foreign imperialism.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military reports
Taiping records and documents
Western diplomatic reports from China

events.certainty_note: The Taiping Rebellion is documented in Qing imperial records and Western diplomatic correspondence. The rebellion continued throughout 1862.

events.title: Victor Hugo publishes 'Les Misérables'
events.description: French author Victor Hugo published 'Les Misérables' in 1862. The novel, one of the greatest works of French literature, tells the story of Jean Valjean and other characters in 19th-century France, exploring themes of justice, redemption, and social inequality. The novel was serialised in a newspaper before being published in book form. 'Les Misérables' became one of the most widely read novels of the 19th century and has remained continuously in print since its publication. The novel's exploration of social injustice and its sympathetic portrayal of the poor made it a powerful critique of French society. The novel has been adapted into numerous stage productions, films, and other works, making it one of the most influential novels in world literature.
key_figures: Gustave Brion (illustrator of the original edition)

sources.name: First edition of 'Les Misérables' (1862)
Hugo's correspondence and manuscripts
Contemporary newspaper reviews and literary journals

events.certainty_note: The publication of 'Les Misérables' is documented in publishing records and contemporary accounts. The date (1862) is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Monitor and Merrimack is often portrayed as a revolutionary moment in naval warfare, but some historians have noted that ironclad warships had been used in earlier conflicts (notably in the Crimean War) and that the battle's significance was more in demonstrating the obsolescence of wooden warships than in introducing ironclad technology. The Battle of Antietam is often cited as a Union victory, but some historians have argued that it was tactically inconclusive and that McClellan's failure to pursue Lee's retreating army represented a missed opportunity. The Homestead Act is often celebrated as a democratic measure that enabled settlers to acquire land, but it also resulted in the systematic dispossession of Native American peoples and the destruction of their way of life. Victor Hugo's 'Les Misérables' is sometimes portrayed as a revolutionary work, but some contemporary critics argued that it was overly sentimental and that its social critique was not sufficiently radical.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1862 is exceptionally rich for American and European events, with extensive documentation of the Civil War battles, the Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation, and the Second French Intervention in Mexico. Western sources dominate the documentation, with particularly detailed coverage of American military campaigns. The Battle of Puebla is well-documented in Mexican and French records, though Mexican perspectives are sometimes underrepresented in English-language sources. The Dakota War is documented in U.S. military records and contemporary accounts, though Dakota perspectives are primarily preserved through oral tradition and later ethnographic accounts. The Taiping Rebellion is documented in Qing imperial records and Western diplomatic correspondence, though Chinese perspectives are sometimes underrepresented in English-language sources. The publication of 'Les Misérables' is well-documented in literary and publishing sources. The year is well-documented overall, with the caveat that non-Western and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented in English-language sources.

graph_edges.note: Battle of the Monitor and Merrimack (March 1862) and Battle of Shiloh (April 1862) were both major Civil War engagements in 1862
Battle of Antietam victory enabled Lincoln to issue Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation
Preliminary Emancipation Proclamation of 1862 led to final Emancipation Proclamation taking effect on January 1, 1863
Second French Intervention in Mexico began in 1862; Battle of Puebla occurred on May 5, 1862
Second French Intervention in Mexico led to installation of Maximilian as Emperor in 1864
Dakota War began in 1862; continued in 1863
Homestead Act signed in May 1862; Dakota War began in August 1862 (both related to westward expansion and Native American displacement)
